title: Your First Commit
subtitle: Saving a snapshot

sections.content: A **commit** is a permanent snapshot of your staged changes. Once you commit, that version of your work is saved in git's history forever. Each commit gets a unique ID and your message describing what changed.
sections.analogy: Committing is like taking a photograph of your desk. The photo captures exactly how everything looks right now. You write a note on the back describing the moment. Later, you can flip through your photos to see how your desk looked at any point in time.
keyPoints: `git commit -m "message"` saves staged changes with a description
Each commit gets a unique hash (ID) that identifies it
Commits are permanent — you can always go back to any previous commit

sections.instruction: You have a project with two files. Let's initialize git and save your first snapshot.
sections.prompt: Type `git init` to start tracking this project.

onSuccess.message: Repository initialized. Now stage your files.

sections.instruction: Stage all files so they're ready to be committed.

onSuccess.message: Both files are staged. Now for the big moment — your first commit.

sections.instruction: A **commit** is a saved snapshot of your project. Every commit needs a message describing what you did. The `-m` flag lets you write that message inline.
sections.prompt: Type `git commit -m "Initial commit"` to save your first snapshot.

onSuccess.message: Your first commit! Git saved a snapshot of your entire project. The message 'Initial commit' describes what this snapshot contains. You can always come back to this exact state.

completionMessage: You made your first commit! The cycle is: make changes, `git add` to stage, `git commit -m` to save. This is the core git workflow you'll use every day.
